content: It was an inspiriting sight, this bright June morning, to see the crowds gathering round Braithwaite's statue.
It was through this alone that the false unity of the Church with its fantastic spiritual fraternity could be counteracted.

Thus he had preluded his speech on the Poor Law question, pointing to the true charity that existed among Masons apart from religious motive, and appealing to the famous benefactions on the Continent; and in the enthusiasm of the Bill's success the Order had received a great accession of members.
Old mrs Brand was in her best to day, and looked out with considerable excitement at the huge throng gathered to hear her son speak.

A platform was erected round the bronze statue at such a height that the statesman appeared to be one of the speakers, though at a slightly higher elevation, and this platform was hung with roses, surmounted by a sounding board, and set with a chair and table.
They had a religious ring; the unintelligent Christian could sing them without a qualm; yet their sense was plain enough-the old human creed that man was all.
The kingdom of God, it was said, lay within the human heart, and the greatest of all graces was Charity.
Then there came a retrospect, comparing the old state of England with the present.
Fifty years ago, the speaker said, poverty was still a disgrace, now it was so no longer.
The second part was to be a panegyric of Braithwaite, treating him as the Precursor of a movement that even now had begun.
Ah! he was working up now to his panegyric!
What was that?
There was a sharp crack, and the tiny gesticulating figure staggered back a step.

Then Oliver was forward again, pointing and crying out, for she could see his gestures; and she sank back quickly, the blood racing through her old veins, and her heart hammering at the base of her throat.
"My dear, my dear, what is it?" she sobbed.
"He was a Catholic," explained the drawn faced Oliver.
"He must have come ready, for his repeater was found loaded.
"He was killed-trampled and strangled instantly," said Oliver.
"I did what I could: you saw me.
"I called out to them, mother, but they wouldn't hear me."
Mabel leaned forward---
Oliver smiled at her.
He knew this tender trait in her.
"It would have been more perfect if they had not," she said.
Then she broke off and sat back.
"Why did he shoot just then?" she asked.

"I said that Braithwaite had done more for the world by one speech than Jesus and all His saints put together." He was aware that the knitting needles stopped for a second; then they went on again as before.
"But he must have meant to do it anyhow," continued Oliver.
"There was a rosary on him; and then he just had time to call on his God."
"And nothing more is known?"
"Nothing more.
He was well dressed, though."
Oliver leaned back a little wearily and closed his eyes; his arm still throbbed intolerably.
But he was very happy at heart.
It was true that he had been wounded by a fanatic, but he was not sorry to bear pain in such a cause, and it was obvious that the sympathy of England was with him.
The huge electric placards over London had winked out the facts in Esperanto as Oliver stepped into the train at twilight.
Catholic assailant....
Indignation of the country....
He was pleased, too, that he honestly had done his best to save the man. Even in that moment of sudden and acute pain he had cried out for a fair trial; but he had been too late.
Oh! there was some passion and loyalty left in England!
"Are you too tired to talk, my dear?"
He opened his eyes.
What is it?"
"What do you think will be the effect?"
"The effect?" he said.
"It can be nothing but good.
It was time that something happened.
Well, I do not think I shall be again.
I have been afraid sometimes that we were losing all our spirit, and that the old Tories were partly right when they prophesied what Communism would do.
But after this---"
"Well?"
"Well; we have shown that we can shed our blood too.
It is in the nick of time, too, just at the crisis.
I don't want to exaggerate; it is only a scratch-but it was so deliberate, and-and so dramatic.
People won't forget it."
"You poor dear!" she said.
"Are you in pain?"
"Not much.
Besides, Christ! what do I care?
He knew he was feverish and irritable, and made a great effort to drive it down.
"If they would not be such heavy fools: they don't understand; they don't understand."
"Yes, Oliver?"
"They don't understand what a glorious thing it all is Humanity, Life, Truth at last, and the death of Folly!
But haven't I told them a hundred times?"
She looked at him with kindling eyes.
She loved to see him like this, his confident, flushed face, the enthusiasm in his blue eyes; and the knowledge of his pain pricked her feeling with passion.
She bent forward and kissed him suddenly.
Oh, Oliver!"

He said nothing; but she could see what she loved to see, that response to her own heart; and so they sat in silence while the sky darkened yet more, and the click of the writer in the next room told them that the world was alive and that they had a share in its affairs.
Oliver stirred presently.
"Did you notice anything just now, sweetheart-when I said that about Jesus Christ?"
"She stopped knitting for a moment," said the girl.
He nodded.
"You saw that too, then....
Mabel, do you think she is falling back?"
"Of course she looks back a little."
"But you don't think-it would be too awful!"
"No, no, my dear; you're excited and tired.
It's just a little sentiment....
After all, she was brought up a Catholic."
She can't get it out of her head, even after fifty years.
Well, watch her, won't you?...
By the way ..."
"Yes?"
They say Felsenburgh's running the whole thing now.
The Empire is sending him everywhere- Tobolsk, Benares, Yakutsk-everywhere; and he's been to Australia."
Mabel sat up briskly.
"Isn't that very hopeful?"
"I suppose so.
There's no doubt that the Sufis are winning; but for how long is another question.
Besides, the troops don't disperse."
"Europe is arming as fast as possible.
I must go."
"Your arm, my dear?"
"My arm must get well.
"There is no more.
But it is just as certain as it can be that this is the crisis.
But if not---"
"Well?"
"If not, there will be a catastrophe such as never has been even imagined.
These new Benninschein explosives will make certain of that."
"But is it absolutely certain that the East has got them?"
"Absolutely.
Benninschein sold them simultaneously to East and West; then he died, luckily for him."
Military experts prophesied extravagantly, contradicting one another on vital points; the whole procedure of war was a matter of theory; there were no precedents with which to compare it.
It was as if archers disputed as to the results of cordite.
But imagination simply refused to speak.
But that was all.
Not many speeches were made on the subject; it had been found inadvisable.
But there was nothing that alarmed her.

She asked the girl to read to her sometimes, and listened unblenching to whatever was offered her; she attended in the kitchen daily, organised varieties of food, and appeared interested in all that concerned her son.
He would be gone three days, he said.
"It is nothing, my dear," said the old lady tremulously; and she added the description of a symptom or two.
"There is no need to telegraph for mr Brand?"
"Well, my dear?" she asked.
It was a peaceful Gospel; at least, it became peaceful as soon as the end had come.
So the girl went downstairs once more, with a quiet little ache at her heart that refused to be still.
She, too, herself would cease one day, let her see to it that the tone was pure and lovely.
"She is a little better, I think," said Mabel.
"She must be very quiet all day."
A couple of hours later, as Mabel went upstairs once more, she met mr Phillips coming down.
"mrs Brand sent for me," he said.
"She wished to know whether mr Oliver would be back to night."
"He will, will he not?
You have not heard?"
"mr Brand said he would be here for a late dinner.
He will reach London at nineteen."
"And is there any other news?"
He compressed his lips.
"There are rumours," he said.
"mr Brand wired to me an hour ago."
He seemed moved at something, and Mabel looked at him in astonishment.
"It is not Eastern news?" she asked.
"You must forgive me, mrs Brand," he said.
She was not offended, for she trusted her husband too well; but she went on into the sick room with her heart beating.
The old lady, too, seemed excited.
"Don't excite yourself, mother.
Oliver will be back to night."
"Don't trouble about me, my dear," she said.
"I shall do very well now. He will be back to dinner, will he not?"
"If the volor is not late.
Now, mother, are you ready for breakfast?"
Mabel passed an afternoon of considerable agitation.
It was certain that something had happened.
The secretary, who breakfasted with her in the parlour looking on to the garden, had appeared strangely excited.
As she came in she took up the evening paper, but there was no news there except to the effect that the Convention would close that afternoon.
Twenty o'clock came, but there was no sign of Oliver.

He had often been later than this: he might have missed the volor he meant to catch; the Convention might have been prolonged; he might be exhausted, and think it better to sleep in Paris after all, and have forgotten to wire. He might even have wired to mr Phillips, and the secretary have forgotten to pass on the message.
She went at last, hopelessly, to the telephone, and looked at it.
Then, even as she turned away, the bell rang sharply, and a white label flashed into sight.--WHITEHALL.
She pressed the corresponding button, and, her hand shaking so much that she could scarcely hold the receiver to her ear, she listened.
"Who is there?"
"Alone here."
Very well.
Can you hear?"
"Yes, yes."
"The best has happened.
Felsenburgh has done it.
Now listen.
We are communicating with the Press. Come up here to me at once.
Can you hear?"
"Oh, yes."
"Come then at once.
Tell no one.
In half an hour the way will be stopped."
"Yes?
Quick."
"Mother is ill.
Shall I leave her?"
"How ill?"
The doctor has seen her."
There was silence for a moment.
" ... Yes, you must come.
Felsenburgh will be there."
